text:
OVERVIEW

Purpose:
This runbook defines the operating model for the Developer Docs site (doc.redwood.internal), the canary content publishing flow, content-owner rotation, and the canonical support-escalation patterns for the top SDK/API issues we see in production. The goal is to reduce mean time to resolution for doc-driven developer problems, keep quickstarts accurate, and create deterministic rollback and remediation paths for site incidents.

Audience:
- Docs authors and maintainers (DX writers, SDK engineers)
- Oncall docs rotation owners
- Developer Support and Solutions Engineering
- Platform and SRE engineers responsible for docs hosting and CDN

GOALS & SUCCESS METRICS
- Reduce doc-related support tickets by 40% for SDK onboarding flows in 90 days.
- Canary publish failure rate < 1% per release.
- Time-to-first-fix for canonical SDK bugs documented in quickstarts: <= 4 hours...

title: Docs site change propagation & developer onramp playbook
preview:

This playbook defines how content changes (snippets, SDK quickstarts, API examples, runbooks) move from authoring to published docs on the public developer docs site and how the developer onramp is kept in sync with canonical fixes and top support issue updates. text:
Summary:

This playbook defines how content changes (snippets, SDK quickstarts, API examples, runbooks) move from authoring to published docs on the public developer docs site and how the developer onramp is kept in sync with canonical fixes and top support issue updates. It codifies roles, automation, verification checks, rollback criteria, and telemetry to ensure we ship accurate, timely developer-facing content.

Scope & audience:
- Scope: content owned by eng-developer-experience (quickstarts, API examples, docs-site pages, canonical fixes). Does NOT replace product PRDs or private customer runbooks living under eng-private-deployments.
- Audience: docs authors, SDK owners, DX PMs, Developer Support, and Docs site SRE.

Definitions:
- Snippet: small code or config fragment included in multiple pages (examples, quickstarts).
- Golden trace: authoritative set of inputs and outputs u...

title: Polyglot adapter patterns, handshake, and telemetry mapping
preview:
Summary:

This page documents an opinionated, production-oriented pattern for building polyglot adapters and SDK shims that integrate client code (Node/Python/Go) with the Redwood inference runtime. The guidance focuses on three areas: reliable handshake and capability negotiation, consistent telemetry mapping for d...
text:
Summary:

This page documents an opinionated, production-oriented pattern for building polyglot adapters and SDK shims that integrate client code (Node/Python/Go) with the Redwood inference runtime. The guidance focuses on three areas: reliable handshake and capability negotiation, consistent telemetry mapping for downstream observability, and a set of canonical fixes for the top support issues we see in adapter integrations.

Intended audience:
- SDK engineers building official Redwood client libraries
- Integration engineers implementing third-party or customer-side adapters
- Developer experience (DX) and support engineers who triage adapter-related incidents

Why this pattern exists:
- We routinely see fragmentation across language adapters (timeouts, retry semantics, telemetry labels) that increases incident MTTR and makes cross-language support expensive.
- A small common contra...

title: Runtime Adapter Playground: Authoring Kit, Telemetry Stubs, and Canary Templates
preview:
Overview

This document describes the Runtime Adapter Playground Authoring Kit — a lightweight, reproducible developer experience for building, testing, and canarying SDK adapters and runtime integrations against Redwood’s local sandbox. The kit bundles:

- opinionated repo layout and templates for new adapters (HTT...
text:
Overview

This document describes the Runtime Adapter Playground Authoring Kit — a lightweight, reproducible developer experience for building, testing, and canarying SDK adapters and runtime integrations against Redwood’s local sandbox. The kit bundles:

- opinionated repo layout and templates for new adapters (HTTP, gRPC, and local shim).
- telemetry stub server and example event schemas so adapters emit stable contract telemetry.
- canary deployment templates and a short runbook for gradual rollout with automated rollback.
- troubleshooting checklist of top support issues and canonical fixes for adapter authors.

Goal and audience

- Goal: Reduce time-to-first-success for adapter authors (internal teams and third-party integrators) by providing a single, documented playground and set of templates that cover the most common failure modes.
- Audience: SDK engineers, integrator partne...

title: Embedded Feature Simulator: SDK Onboarding Examples & Telemetry Codelets
preview:
Overview

This page describes a lightweight, reproducible simulator pattern for embedding feature flows used in SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and support triage. The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, s...
text:
Overview

This page describes a lightweight, reproducible simulator pattern for embedding feature flows used in SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and support triage. The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, streaming, and local telemetry emission. It is intended to help SDK authors, support engineers, and sample-app integrators validate behavior against Redwood API expectations before full integration.

Why this exists

- Speed: launch a local stub that reproduces class of SDK runtime problems in under a minute.
- Determinism: control tokenization and response timing to exercise batching and timeout logic.
- Observability alignment: validate telemetry event formats and correlation IDs before customers escalate.
- Canonical fixes: include reproducible codelets that match support...

title: SDK telemetry mismatch — canonical detection & fixes
preview:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, de...
text:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, developer-experience support, and oncall SREs.

Why this matters
------------------

Telemetry fidelity is required for accurate cost attribution, latency breakdowns, and automatic routing decisions. Mismatches lead to:
- Incorrect token counts in billing dashboards
- Misattributed latency or error spikes that obscure root cause
- Invalid sampling for analytics or A/B rollouts
- Failed dependability checks in canary deployments

Common symptom categories
-------------------------

1) Token count delta — SDK reports fewer/more tokens than server: typically caused by encoding d...

title: Mini Emulator + Telemetry Mirror Playbook for SDK Onboarding
preview:
Summary

This playbook explains the 'mini emulator' pattern we use for rapid SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and Support first-response debugging. The emulator is a lightweight, single-process stub that reproduces key runtime behaviors of Redwood's hosted inference API: streaming, batching (approximate beha...
text:
Summary

This playbook explains the 'mini emulator' pattern we use for rapid SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and Support first-response debugging. The emulator is a lightweight, single-process stub that reproduces key runtime behaviors of Redwood's hosted inference API: streaming, batching (approximate behavior), token accounting, configurable latency, and an optional telemetry mirror that emits a compact golden trace for deterministic replay. The goal is fast developer feedback loops and a reproducible first-level-support workflow.

Intended audience

- SDK engineers authoring client libraries
- Developer Experience (DevX) engineers creating quickstarts and templates
- Support engineers doing rapid reproduction and canonical fixes

Motivation

New integrators often get stuck on three recurring issues: (1) streaming/diffing mismatches, (2) token-count and pricing surprises, a...

title: Developer Shield: SDK Chaos & Recovery Lab
preview:
Summary:
This lab is a hands-on developer-ready guide for intentionally breaking SDK integrations, observing telemetry "golden traces", and validating recovery strategies against Redwood inference endpoints. The goal is to provide a repeatable workshop that SDK authors, platform engineers, and support oncall can run...
text:
Summary:
This lab is a hands-on developer-ready guide for intentionally breaking SDK integrations, observing telemetry "golden traces", and validating recovery strategies against Redwood inference endpoints. The goal is to provide a repeatable workshop that SDK authors, platform engineers, and support oncall can run in ~90 minutes to verify resilience SLA behaviors and canonical fixes.

Goals:
- Create deterministic fault patterns targeting SDK -> runtime interactions (timeouts, partial responses, truncated streams).
- Validate observability: traces, spans, logs, and token-level cost telemetry for each failure mode.
- Exercise client-side fallbacks (retry jitter, streaming resume, local cache) and server-side graceful degradation (model variant fallback, rate-aware routing).
- Produce documented artifacts: golden-trace examples, regression tests, and a triage checklist for support.

P...

title: Streaming quickstart (standardized SDK behavior: events, timeouts, disconnects, retries)
preview:
# Streaming quickstart (consistent behavior across Python, TypeScript, Go)

This quickstart shows the **standardized streaming behavior** implemented across Redwood SDKs (Python / TypeScript / Go) as part of the SDK parity initiative.

It covers:
- Standard **streaming event model** (what you receive and how to asse...
text:
# Streaming quickstart (consistent behavior across Python, TypeScript, Go)

This quickstart shows the **standardized streaming behavior** implemented across Redwood SDKs (Python / TypeScript / Go) as part of the SDK parity initiative.

It covers:
- Standard **streaming event model** (what you receive and how to assemble output)
- **Timeout configuration** for streaming vs non-streaming
- **Disconnect handling** and what “partial output” means
- Recommended **retry patterns** (including when *not* to retry)
- Required logging fields for easier support/triage

## 1) Prerequisites

### Authentication
All SDKs use the same auth resolution order:
1) Explicit API key / token passed to the client constructor
2) Environment variables

Recommended environment variable:
- `REDWOOD_API_KEY`

Notes:
- Do **not** log API keys or tokens. SDKs redact secrets in errors and debug logs by default.
- Re...

title: Console Docs (Internal Draft): Optimization Suggestions (Beta)
preview:
## Overview
Optimization Suggestions (Beta) is a Redwood Console experience that highlights *batching* and *caching* configuration opportunities to reduce inference cost while managing latency/reliability risk.

Suggestions are generated from your recent telemetry (traffic, latency distribution, cache metrics, and c...
text:
## Overview
Optimization Suggestions (Beta) is a Redwood Console experience that highlights *batching* and *caching* configuration opportunities to reduce inference cost while managing latency/reliability risk.

Suggestions are generated from your recent telemetry (traffic, latency distribution, cache metrics, and concurrency signals) and presented per **route + model**.

This beta focuses on:
- Recommendations for **continuous batching** and **prefix/KV caching** settings
- Estimates for:
 - **Savings** (e.g., $/day and %)
 - **Performance impact** (p50/p95 latency deltas)
 - A simple **risk level** (Low/Medium/High) with plain-language explanations
- A “closed loop” that records what you viewed/dismissed/applied so we can improve suggestion quality

Non-goals (beta):
- Quantization recommendations UI (may be referenced, not surfaced as suggestions here)
- Auto-applying changes witho...

title: SDK Migration Notes: Standardized Auth, Retries, and Timeouts (Python/TypeScript/Go)
preview:
## Summary
As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative, Redwood’s Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs now follow the same defaults and configuration model for:
- Authentication resolution (explicit credentials vs environment variables)
- Retry policy (which errors retry, backoff/jitter behavior, Retry-After)
- T...
text:
## Summary
As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative, Redwood’s Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs now follow the same defaults and configuration model for:
- Authentication resolution (explicit credentials vs environment variables)
- Retry policy (which errors retry, backoff/jitter behavior, Retry-After)
- Timeout model (connect/read/overall semantics)
- Streaming semantics (disconnect behavior and retry guidance)

These changes reduce “works in TS but not Python/Go” mismatches and improve reliability under elevated 429/5xx conditions.

## Who should read this
Read this guide if you:
- Upgraded to the latest major/minor SDK release in any language
- Set custom retries/timeouts previously (especially for streaming)
- Rely on environment variables for credentials
- Have a corporate proxy/load balancer that can interrupt streaming

## Compatibility & versioning
This page describe...

text:
## Purpose
This page is the working parity matrix for the cross-language SDK parity initiative. It documents **current behavior** and **target behavior** across:
- **redwood-sdk-python**
- **redwood-sdk-typescript**
- **redwood-sdk-go**

The matrix is used as:
1) the source-of-truth for what “parity” means for auth/retry/timeout/streaming/error behavior,
2) the checklist for code changes and docs updates, and
3) the basis for the cross-language conformance test suite.

## Scope
In scope:
- API key / bearer token auth resolution order, env var naming, headers
- Retry policy: retryable status codes, network errors, backoff/jitter, respect Retry-After
- Timeout model: connect/read/overall semantics and defaults
- Streaming behavior: disconnects, partial output, cancellation, retry guidance
- Error taxonomy: typed errors + standardized fields (request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, etc.)

O...

text:
Overview

This runbook describes the recommended, production-tested process for migrating an integration from direct Redwood API calls to our first-class SDKs (or vice-versa), the telemetry contract that SDKs must emit, and canonical fixes for the most common support issues encountered during migration. The goal is to reduce developer friction, minimize regressions in latency/cost, and provide a reproducible set of artifacts that docs writers, SDK engineers, and support can use during onboarding and incident triage.

Audience

- Application engineers migrating to/from Redwood SDKs
- SDK maintainers
- DevEx writers and on-call support engineers

Scope and assumptions

- Covers text-generation, streaming, and embeddings use-cases.
- Assumes target Redwood SDK version >= 2.4.0 (contains unified telemetry hooks).
- Covers both hosted (Redwood API) and Dedicated/Private endpoints where beh...

title: SDK Parity Initiative (Python/TypeScript/Go) — Audit Overview
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is running a cross-language **SDK parity** initiative to ensure the **Python, TypeScript, and Go** SDKs behave consistently and predictably in production, with particular focus on:
- Authentication resolution and header formatting
- Retries / backoff / Retry-After handling
- Timeouts (ov...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is running a cross-language **SDK parity** initiative to ensure the **Python, TypeScript, and Go** SDKs behave consistently and predictably in production, with particular focus on:
- Authentication resolution and header formatting
- Retries / backoff / Retry-After handling
- Timeouts (overall vs connect/read) and cancellation
- Streaming semantics (disconnect behavior, partial output, and retry guidance)
- Error taxonomy (typed errors and consistent fields such as request_id)

This page is the published overview for stakeholders and contributors. For the detailed current-state findings and acceptance criteria, see the parity matrix.

---

## Why we are doing this
Customer friction and support volume are disproportionately driven by “works in TS but not in Python” inconsistencies. The most common failure modes we are addressing:
- 429 rate limits handled di...

text:
Summary:
This playbook consolidates recommended SDK patterns for streaming + batching usage with Redwood Inference APIs, a short set of quickstart templates for client teams, and canonical fixes for the top support issues we observe in the field. The goal is to reduce token cost without compromising latency SLOs and to provide reproducible steps for common incidents.

Scope:
- SDKs covered: python, node, and go (patterns are portable).
- Traffic modes: low-latency single-request streaming, throughput-oriented batched synchronous requests, hybrid streaming-with-batch setups.
- Not covered: model tuning, model architecture changes.

Intended audience:
- Application engineers integrating Redwood APIs.
- SDK maintainers and support engineers.

text:
## Purpose
This page summarizes the highest-volume and highest-severity Support themes observed in Q1 2026 (to date) and recommends documentation/UX changes to reduce time-to-triage and repeat tickets. It also records the shortlist selected for the Feb 2026 “known-issues sprint” and the rationale for prioritization.

This document is written for DevEx/Docs, Support Ops, and engineering partners (SRE/Platform/Runtime). It is intentionally customer-safe (no customer names, no contract terms beyond tier-level policy, no sensitive incident details).

---

## Time window + data sources
**Time window analyzed:** 2026-01-01 through 2026-02-12 (rolling Q1 view; refreshed during sprint review on 2026-02-14).

**Data sources:**
- Jira Support project (SUP tickets) with Priority (P0–P4), Tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private/Enterprise), Region, Model, and “Repeat issue?” flags.

title: SDK Back-compatibility & Deprecation Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines Redwood's expectations, guardrails, and operational steps for SDK backward compatibility, controlled deprecation, and developer-facing migration tooling. It is intended for SDK maintainers, release engineers, developer docs authors, and support engineers who help customers migrate whe...
text:
Overview

This playbook defines Redwood's expectations, guardrails, and operational steps for SDK backward compatibility, controlled deprecation, and developer-facing migration tooling. It is intended for SDK maintainers, release engineers, developer docs authors, and support engineers who help customers migrate when an SDK behavior or API surface changes.

Goals
- Keep customer integrations stable: preserve commonly relied-on behavior across minor/patch releases.
- Provide predictable migration windows and clear communication for breaking changes.
- Deliver measurable tests and automated migrations where possible.
- Minimize support escalations with pre-release checks, canaries, and compatibility monitors.

Scope
- Applies to all Redwood client SDKs (JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Go, Java, .NET) published by the Developer Experience team.
- Includes public SDK APIs, CLI tools distri...

Overview

Purpose
This playbook describes a low-friction, repeatable approach for adopting lightweight SDK instrumentation for Redwood Inference customers and internal integrators. It is targeted at SDK owners, integrators, and Developer Experience engineers who want to add dependable telemetry with minimal code changes.

Goals
- Minimize developer effort to get meaningful telemetry (3–5 events + error traces).
- Provide clear sampling, retention, and PII/redaction guidance so telemetry is safe by default.
- Offer canonical fixes for top support issues encountered during SDK telemetry onboarding.
- Provide a short canary/rollout path with monitoring and rollback guidance.

Audience
- SDK engineers and maintainers (Python, Node, Java)
- DevX engineers who help customers integrate Redwood APIs
- Support engineers responsible for triage and canonical fixes

title: SDK Patch Release Playbook and Compatibility Rules
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes the lightweight, repeatable process we use to ship SDK patch releases (hotfixes, security patches, backward-compatible minor fixes) and the compatibility rules that must be enforced by the devx team. The goal is to minimize user impact, ensure consistent docs/website updates, and p...
text:
Overview:

This playbook describes the lightweight, repeatable process we use to ship SDK patch releases (hotfixes, security patches, backward-compatible minor fixes) and the compatibility rules that must be enforced by the devx team. The goal is to minimize user impact, ensure consistent docs/website updates, and provide a clear triage + rollback path for incidents tied to SDK releases.

When to use this playbook:
- Patches for regressions in Official Redwood SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go) that affect runtime correctness or cause high-severity developer experience regressions (errors in auth, streaming breaking, incorrect typing).
- Security fixes in dependency tree that require immediate release.
- Minor quality-of-life fixes that need to go out in the next patch window (semver PATCH).

High-level constraints and rules:
1) Semver policy: follow semver strictly. Patches must be releas...

Overview

This playbook documents the standard approach for producing compact SDK builds and lightweight runtime stubs used by Redwood customer integrations and internal sample apps. It targets two immediate goals: minimize client-side artifact size for web and edge consumers, and provide deterministic runtime stubs for local development and CI that reproduce production behavior without requiring a full runtime backend.

Audience

- SDK engineers responsible for packaging and releases
- Developer advocates creating sample apps and quickstarts
- Support engineers shipping canonical fixes for client-side issues

When to use this playbook

- New SDK languages or client bindings are being introduced
- Bundle size regressions are detected in CI (>20% increase)
- Customers report long cold-load times for browser-based SDKs

title: Hosted API: Overload errors + retry guidance (Docs plan and content outline)
preview:
## Overview
This page is the doc plan and content outline for updating the public developer documentation to cover the new overload protection behavior in the Hosted API (circuit breakers + load shedding). The goal is to make overload behavior predictable for customers and reduce retry storms during regional saturat...
text:
## Overview
This page is the doc plan and content outline for updating the public developer documentation to cover the new overload protection behavior in the Hosted API (circuit breakers + load shedding). The goal is to make overload behavior predictable for customers and reduce retry storms during regional saturation or dependency brownouts.

This doc update is intentionally scoped to:
- Customer-visible error semantics (HTTP status + stable error codes)
- JSON error payload shape (additive fields)
- Recommended client retry behavior (including `Retry-After` semantics)
- Idempotency and safe retries (especially for POST + streaming)
- Concrete examples and SDK notes

Non-goals:
- Pricing/billing policy changes
- Redesigning the SDK surface area beyond error typing and basic helpers

## Target publication
- Docs location: Developer Docs → Hosted API → “Errors” + “Reliability” section...

text:
Overview

Purpose: This document defines recommended SDK design patterns for tenant-scoped authentication and robust local fallbacks. Its goal is to reduce support incidents and production outages in multi-tenant applications that call Redwood inference endpoints. The guidance is written for SDK implementers (client libraries and server-side helper libs) and for developer-facing code patterns used by product teams.

Intended audience:
- SDK engineers implementing Redwood client libraries
- Platform engineers integrating tenant-aware routing
- Support engineers troubleshooting tenant-impacted incidents

Why this matters
- Multi-tenant workloads commonly fail in two categories: auth/identity mispropagation and global rate-limit storms.
- Proper tenant-scoped tokens, local caches, and graceful fallbacks prevent noisy neighbor effects and provide predictable user experience when upstream ...

title: API deterministic output samples and canonical fixes

Overview

It is intended for SDK authors, integration engineers, and support engineers who triage output variance regressions or...
text:
Overview

This guide documents patterns, quickstart snippets, telemetry hooks, and canonical fixes for achieving reproducible (deterministic) API outputs when using Redwood SDKs and the Redwood API. It is intended for SDK authors, integration engineers, and support engineers who triage output variance regressions or write tests/benchmarks that require repeatable responses.

Why deterministic outputs matter

- Evals and regression tests depend on repeatable responses when measuring model drift or SDK changes.
- Customer integrations (prompt templates, scoring) sometimes rely on stable tokenization and sampling outputs for business logic.
- Determinism reduces flakiness in CI and helps isolate runtime vs model vs prompt issues.

Scope and assumptions

- Applies to text generation APIs (chat/generate/stream) and to SDKs wrapping those endpoints.
- Does not attempt to override model-level...

title: Production Readiness: SDK Recommendations (timeouts, retries, idempotency, streaming)
preview:
## Overview
This page defines Redwood-recommended **production defaults** for Redwood SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go) and the OpenAI-compat layer, with emphasis on:
- **Timeouts** that fail fast but don’t break streaming.
- **Retries/backoff** that reduce user-facing errors without amplifying load.
- **Idempotency** t...
text:
## Overview
This page defines Redwood-recommended **production defaults** for Redwood SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go) and the OpenAI-compat layer, with emphasis on:
- **Timeouts** that fail fast but don’t break streaming.
- **Retries/backoff** that reduce user-facing errors without amplifying load.
- **Idempotency** to prevent accidental duplicate requests and double processing.
- **Streaming disconnect handling** patterns that are safe and predictable.

These recommendations are used by:
- The in-Console **Guided Onboarding / First Production Launch** checklist.
- Quickstarts and snippet generators.
- Support triage macros.

## Non-goals
- This is not a full resiliency guide (circuit breakers, bulkheads, queueing). See SRE/Platform standards for those.
- This does not define backend behavior; it defines **client expectations** and safe defaults.

---

## Principles (what “production-sa...

## Summary
This document summarizes the highest-volume onboarding-related support failures observed in Q1 2025 (Jan 1–Mar 31) for Hosted API customers, and maps each failure mode to mitigations delivered (or planned) via:
- Console “Guided Onboarding + First Production Launch” checklist (MVP)
- Docs updates and information architecture changes
- SDK “production-safe defaults” (timeouts/retries/streaming resilience)
- Telemetry/eventing improvements to reduce time-to-diagnosis and measure impact

Primary goal: reduce avoidable tickets and reduce time-to-first-success (TTFS) + time-to-first-production-traffic (TTFP) by making common pitfalls explicit and self-service.

## Data sources + methodology
**Data sources**
- Jira Support tickets labeled: onboarding, quickstart, auth, rate-limit, streaming, usage-dashboard (Q1 2025).

text:
## Summary
This page defines the documentation information architecture (IA) changes required to support the **Guided Onboarding + First Production Launch Checklist** in the Redwood Console.

It maps each checklist step to:
- A **canonical docs page** (and where applicable, a stable **section anchor** for deep linking)
- **Ownership/DRI** for ongoing maintenance
- **Redirects** from legacy /getting-started and quickstart pages to prevent broken links and reduce duplicate guidance

This IA is written to be implementable in the `redwood-docs` repo navigation + redirects configuration and to support Console deep links.

## Goals
1. Provide a single “source of truth” docs path for each checklist step so the Console can deep-link to docs consistently.
2. Reduce top onboarding-related tickets by making troubleshooting and production-safe defaults easy to find:
 - 401/403 auth mistakes (API ...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **canonical step catalog** for the “Guided onboarding + First Production Launch” checklist.

This template is the **single source of truth** for:
- Step IDs (stable identifiers used by Console + progress service + telemetry)
- Step ordering, dependencies, and completion criteria
- Deep links in the Redwood Console
- Canonical docs + quickstart/example links (with language parity expectations)

This template is used in:
- **Redwood Console** checklist UI
- **Docs** (mirrors the same steps and order)
- **Support playbooks** (triage by step state)

---

## Template versioning and change control
**Template version:** 1.0

**Rules**
1. **Step IDs are immutable.** Do not reuse an ID for a different meaning.
2. Any change that affects completion logic (criteria or event mapping) requires review from:
 - DevEx (Docs/Quickstarts): Wei Chen / Elliot Price
 - Con...

title: Evals and Regression Testing for Quickstart Templates
preview:
## Overview
This document defines how Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents, Python and TypeScript) are evaluated and regression-tested. The intent is to catch:
- Functional regressions (retrieval correctness, tool calling behavior, structured output parsing)
- Reliability regressions (streaming disco...
text:
## Overview
This document defines how Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents, Python and TypeScript) are evaluated and regression-tested. The intent is to catch:
- Functional regressions (retrieval correctness, tool calling behavior, structured output parsing)
- Reliability regressions (streaming disconnect handling, retries/backoff, timeouts)
- Breaking changes in SDKs/dependencies that impact template users
- Observability regressions (missing spans/metrics, log redaction failures)

We implement a layered strategy:
1) **Golden prompt sets** (deterministic-ish behavioral checks)
2) **Offline eval harness integration** (repeatable scoring and trending)
3) **Smoke tests** (streaming + tools + basic RAG end-to-end)
4) **CI quality gates** (release blocking where appropriate)

## Goals
- Make template updates safe to ship without requiring maintainers to manually validate e...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood Inference ships and maintains Quickstart Templates (RAG + Agents, etc.) across languages, including:
- Versioning policy (templates + scaffolding CLI)
- Changelog expectations
- Deprecation strategy
- Step-by-step instructions for cutting a release
- How docs link to “latest” vs “pinned” template versions

The goal is to keep templates:
- Safe by default (security + privacy)
- Stable for production use (repeatable scaffolds, predictable upgrades)
- Easy to maintain (CI gates, reproducible builds)

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Template repositories under the Redwood org (e.g., `redwood-quickstarts`)
- The scaffolding CLI used to initialize templates (e.g., `redwood-templates`)
- Docs-site integration (how we link templates and recommend versions)

**Out of scope:**
- SDK release processes (tracked in SDK repos)
- Observability Pack release process (...

title: Route Experiments (A/B testing) – SDK integration notes (stickiness keys, headers, docs plan)

- How customers provide **deterministic stickiness keys** (so a user consistently receives the same variant)

text:
## Purpose
These notes describe how Redwood SDKs and examples should integrate with **Route Experiments** (route-level A/B/n testing in Console Rollouts), with specific focus on:
- How customers provide **deterministic stickiness keys** (so a user consistently receives the same variant)
- How SDKs should expose and document **experiment/variant response headers**
- A documentation + examples plan for beta → GA

This page is DX-facing (SDKs, docs, examples). It is not the source of truth for routing semantics; see ADR-047 / ADR-048.

---

## Customer-facing mental model (what we want users to understand)
1. An experiment is attached to a **route** (endpoint / routing policy).
2. Each request is assigned to a **variant** based on a stable **stickiness key**.
3. Redwood returns optional **debug headers** so customers can tell which variant they hit.
4. Console shows per-variant metrics (...

title: Template Requirements (v1): RAG + Agents Quickstarts
preview:

The goal is to ship templates that are production-minded by default: resilient streaming, safe configuration, observable behavior, and a clear “golden path” fr...
text:
# Overview
This page defines the **mandatory requirements and acceptance criteria** for Redwood’s **RAG + agent** quickstart templates (Python + TypeScript). The goal is to ship templates that are production-minded by default: resilient streaming, safe configuration, observable behavior, and a clear “golden path” from sign-up to a working deployment.

This document is written for template authors, SDK owners, reviewers (SRE/Observability/Security), and Docs.

---

# Goals
- Provide a **reference implementation** of a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline plus a minimal agent loop that uses **tool calling**.
- Ensure templates are **operationally supportable**: metrics, traces, structured logs, error taxonomy, and correlation IDs.
- Ensure templates are **safe by default**: timeouts, retries/backoff, bounded cost controls, redaction, and no insecure secret handling.
- Keep temp...

1) abstract LLM providers to minimize lock-in and enable local/enterprise routing, and
2) pin models and versions safely so template apps are reproducible, rollout-friendly, and supportable.

The guidance applies to RAG and age...
text:
## Summary
This page defines how Redwood quickstart templates (Python + TypeScript) must:
1) abstract LLM providers to minimize lock-in and enable local/enterprise routing, and
2) pin models and versions safely so template apps are reproducible, rollout-friendly, and supportable.

The guidance applies to RAG and agent templates (including tool calling + structured output). It also covers when and how we support Redwood’s OpenAI-compatible surface.

---

## Goals
- **Reproducibility:** A template scaffold created today should behave the same tomorrow (within documented bounds).
- **Rollout safety:** Provide a straightforward way to do canaries / A/B tests and controlled upgrades.
- **Provider flexibility:** Allow swapping providers (Redwood hosted, Redwood Dedicated/Private endpoints, OpenAI-compatible) with minimal code changes.
- **Supportability:** Make it easy for Support/SRE to id...

title: Quickstart Templates: Architecture Overview (RAG + Agents)
preview:
## Purpose
This page describes the shared architecture for Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (initially Python + TypeScript), with a focus on production-minded defaults for RAG and agentic workflows.

The goal is a consistent shape across templates so that:
- Developers can move from one template to another wit...
text:
## Purpose
This page describes the shared architecture for Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (initially Python + TypeScript), with a focus on production-minded defaults for RAG and agentic workflows.

The goal is a consistent shape across templates so that:
- Developers can move from one template to another without re-learning concepts.
- Observability signals are uniform and “supportable” (we can debug issues quickly).
- Provider abstraction remains clean (minimal lock-in) while still showcasing Redwood-specific best practices.

## Non-goals
- This is not a full product spec for the Hosted API, Dedicated, or Private control planes.
- This is not a deep reference for OpenTelemetry or vector database internals.
- We do not define a new agent framework; templates include a small, readable agent loop with clear extension points.

## Template family overview
We ship a small set of te...

title: Quickstart Templates: Safe Defaults and Guardrails (RAG + Agents)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **safe defaults and guardrails** that all Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents) must ship with. The goal is to help developers get to production-quality behavior by default:
- resilient networking (timeouts, retries, streaming robustness)
- predictable cost controls (...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **safe defaults and guardrails** that all Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents) must ship with. The goal is to help developers get to production-quality behavior by default:
- resilient networking (timeouts, retries, streaming robustness)
- predictable cost controls (token caps, concurrency caps)
- safe tool usage (schema validation, bounded loops)
- security-minded handling of prompts, retrieved content, and PII
- clear, actionable fallbacks when dependencies fail

This doc is normative for templates. Where language/runtime differences exist (Python vs TypeScript), templates must match **intent** and **effective behavior**, even if implementation details differ.

## Non-goals
- This is not a comprehensive app security guide.
- This does not define customer-specific policy (e.g., sector compliance); it defines baseline defaults suitable...

title: Quickstart Templates Observability Hooks Standard (OTel + Redwood Conventions)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines the **required observability hooks** for Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents, Python + TypeScript). Templates must emit consistent **metrics, traces, and logs** so that:
- Users can validate correct behavior (streaming, retrieval, tool-calling) quickly.
- Redwood Sup...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines the **required observability hooks** for Redwood Inference Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents, Python + TypeScript). Templates must emit consistent **metrics, traces, and logs** so that:
- Users can validate correct behavior (streaming, retrieval, tool-calling) quickly.
- Redwood Support/SRE can debug issues with minimal back-and-forth.
- Product/Analytics can segment template-originated traffic (where allowed) without collecting sensitive payloads.

This standard is implemented via the **Template Observability Pack** (OTel collector config + dashboards + alert recommendations) and lightweight template libraries/wrappers.

## Scope
Applies to all template apps shipped by DevEx:
- RAG API template
- RAG UI template (if applicable)
- Agent loop with tool calling
- Batch embeddings / ingestion jobs (where present)

Non-goals:
- Defining full org-wide OTel...

title: SDK Contract-First Local Dev Loop Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook documents our recommended contract-first approach to SDK integration and the local developer loop. It is intended for SDK authors, platform integrators, and application engineers who want a reproducible way to:

- bootstrap a new SDK integration using a machine-readable contract,
- iterate ra...
text:
Overview

This playbook documents our recommended contract-first approach to SDK integration and the local developer loop. It is intended for SDK authors, platform integrators, and application engineers who want a reproducible way to:

- bootstrap a new SDK integration using a machine-readable contract,
- iterate rapidly in a local environment that mirrors Redwood hosted behavior,
- ship reliable instrumentation and tests that catch API drift early, and
- provide canonical fixes for common developer support issues.

Why contract-first?

Working from a contract (OpenAPI/JSON Schema/IDLs) reduces misunderstanding between runtime, client SDKs, and product teams. It enables automated generation of request/response shapes, validation, test scaffolding, and a local mock server that can be shipped as part of the SDK developer experience.

Audience and scope

- Audience: SDK maintainers, inte...
